text: The surgical field reveals a bulky sigmoid colon with multiple vascular malformations, including angiomas involving the spleen and liver.
The surgeon begins dissection through an avascular portion of the sigmoid colon, carefully navigating around visible vascular structures to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon and uses ultrasonic scissors to dissect and divide a relatively avascular portion of the sigmoid colon.
A window is created in the tissue, and a cotton tape is passed through it for traction, avoiding sharp instruments to minimize bleeding at the traction sites.
The surgeon ties the colon with a cotton tape, using the tie to apply traction for better manipulation and visualization during the procedure.
A cotton tape is tied around the rectosigmoid region and used as traction to manipulate the colon during the procedure.
The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon and places additional retracting ports to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon uses suction cannulae to retract the sigmoid colon, avoiding sharp instruments to prevent bleeding from the traction sites.
The surgeon performs further dissection through the avascular mesocolon of the rectosigmoid, progressing towards the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies angiomas on the pelvic side walls and within the mesorectum. A relatively avascular plane in the mesorectum is carefully chosen for downward dissection.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic scissors to carefully dissect towards the pelvis, with visible angiomas in the pelvic side walls and mesorectum.
The surgeon directs the dissection towards the left side of the patient, carefully dividing the left pelvic peritoneum while avoiding injury to the ureter.
The surgeon visualizes the ureter through the peritoneal reflection, aided by the patient's thin body habitus, ensuring careful dissection to avoid injury.
The surgeon divides the sigmoid mesentery near the mesocolic vessels, carefully separating the tissue in this region.
The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides the individual sigmoid branches, preparing to resect the entire involved sigmoid colon and rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pelvis, aiming to reach deeper into the pelvic region, using ultrasonic scissors to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon continues dissection of the lower rectum below the peritoneal reflection, focusing on the area beneath the previously divided left pelvic peritoneum.
The surgeon performs dissection of the posterior rectum, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic sutures to carefully maintain the dissection plane, which contains minimal vascular tissues, ensuring precise separation of anatomical structures.
The dissection progresses close to the pelvic floor, revealing the colon's proximity to this area, which may impact the available length for a successful anastomosis.
The surgeon mobilizes the normal descending colon and the splenic flexure, preparing these sections for further manipulation and potential anastomosis.
The surgeon performs dissection using a monopolar hook, navigating through relatively normal tissue planes in the lower rectum below the peritoneal reflection.
The surgeon mobilizes the entire sigmoid colon, maneuvering it downward towards the pelvis to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the final dissection in the lower pelvis, carefully separating tissue layers to ensure adequate colon length for anastomosis.
The surgeon develops the posterior mesoacral plane down to the low pelvis, advancing the dissection towards the pelvic floor.
The surgeon divides the lateral tissues in the pelvic region, carefully avoiding injury to the visible autonomic nerves during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies multiple vessels located in the lateral pelvic wall and predominantly in the anterior space between the rectum and bladder.
The surgeon divides the rectovesicle peritoneum, separating it from the surrounding structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum below the peritoneal reflection, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues in the lower pelvis.
The surgeon carefully dissects beneath the peritoneum, navigating around multiple visible vessels to avoid injury.
The surgeon tapers the mesorectum at the decided level of transection using a harmonic scalpel and a bipolar instrument, carefully manipulating the tissue to ensure precise dissection.

The surgeon navigates a challenging phase due to bleeding from multiple vessels and thickened tissues, likely from recurrent inflammation. The rectal wall appears thicker than usual as the rectum is tapered circumferentially. A 15mm trocar is introduced into the identified transduction plane.
The surgeon applies a black, sixty millimeter thick cartridge to the rectum for transduction, following the division of the rectovesicle peritoneum.
The surgeon uses a thick cartridge to safely perform transduction on the thickened rectal tissues, ensuring secure closure and cutting.
The surgeon divides the rectum, completing the transection at the predetermined level, with visible separation of the rectal tissue.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the surgical field, ensuring control of bleeding from multiple vessels encountered during the procedure.

The surgeon brings the descending colon and sigmoid colon into the pelvis to assess the length and sufficiency of the anastomosis. The proximal margin of the relatively normal colon is marked, and the specimen is extracted through a phanastial incision.
The surgeon performs a straight colorectal anastomosis under direct vision, connecting the descending colon to the rectum.
The surgeon completes a tension-free anastomosis of the rectum and colon, ensuring proper alignment and secure connection of the bowel segments.
